content:
Modern **cross-validation** traces to **M. Stone's** early work on **cross-validatory assessment** (1974) and the broader **resampling** tradition in statistics. Before affordable compute, analysts relied on a single hold-out sample; as models and hyperparameter grids grew, reusing data through rotating validation folds became standard practice.

**k-fold CV** as taught in ML courses popularized averaging k train/val rotations—often **k = 10** following empirical studies suggesting a stable bias–variance tradeoff for many tabular tasks. **Leave-one-out** variants appeared earlier in classification error estimation but remain costly at scale.

**Stratified** folding adapted CV to **imbalanced classification**, preserving class counts per fold much like stratified hold-out splits.

**Nested cross-validation** formalized the distinction between **selection** (inner loop) and **evaluation** (outer loop), addressing optimism when the same CV scores both tune and report performance.

Libraries such as **scikit-learn** (`cross_val_score`, `StratifiedKFold`, `GridSearchCV` with CV) made the protocol one line in Python pipelines—yet the interpretive rules (preprocessing per fold, locked test sets, group/time structure) remain the responsibility of the practitioner.

timeline.label: Cross-validatory assessment (1970s)
timeline.description: Stone and contemporaries formalize rotating validation as an alternative to single hold-out estimation.

timeline.label: k-fold becomes ML default
timeline.description: Empirical studies and textbooks adopt k=5 or k=10 as practical defaults for tabular model comparison.

timeline.label: Stratified folding
timeline.description: Class-preserving folds extend CV to imbalanced classification without empty validation slices.

timeline.label: Nested CV and library integration
timeline.description: Nested loops separate tuning from evaluation; sklearn exposes splitters and cross_val_score in unified APIs.
